Twitch plate. Hey, Rican, make him sit down and shut up. I gotta say something. Isn't it nice to learn something from somebody who really takes the bullshit out of his play? Okay, speaking of that, we're going to... I'm going to take just a pause and see if people have questions about what we talked about last time. Just a few minutes for questions if there are any questions. Yes? No, you're not, but I bet you Terry would probably Z-Rox and send you them. I'm sure that wasn't part of the package. Not you, but I'll send them to you. What did you want? You need to go back to Bern, Stevenson, whatever, and use his name as if he's writing it himself and get his permission to do that. Well, you certainly can't do it without his permission. That's true. You must have it. I'm not almost writing it, but it's his story. Yes, and you don't always have him sign it. You could write about him or you could have him write it. Probably, if you had to make a choice in the decision, it's probably usually better to have the person sign the letter that's writing it. That's probably better. Any other questions about this? I don't know if you're crossing the line between using something as a template and plagiarizing something. Well, I think that's what we should do. That's all one of the best things about this. Everybody has their own ethical set of values. I think you will know who you're going over the line. Some people, their copywriter, his name is Xerox. I saw a guy copy Joe Carbo's ad one time, and virtually the only thing he's changed was the address where you sent the money and the name of the person you sent it to. I have people who copy my stuff so closely that it's offensive. I actually, sometimes when they write newsletters, I'm embarrassed for it. I think that you should be inspired by other people's work, and you can use it as a template, but you'll know who you're over the line. The thing is, you don't really have to steal. You don't even want to. You want to use it to be inspired and make it fresh. Anybody else have a question? I have a question. You like the way you play. Do you play last when I put down the piano? They've almost come into public domain. Well, I think, actually, do you know, just in case you're wondering, you cannot copyright a headline and you cannot copyright a title to a book. Did you know that? You know that you could write a book, a title, and it's gone with the win. The test, however, is whether or not what you have done could easily be confused with the best seller gone with the win. Like if you wrote a book called Gone with the Win and made the cover and made the whole thing premise look like they'd get you. And I don't necessarily think using a headline, almost word for word, would be too awful. I just, you know, but I don't, you really don't need to do that. You can make some changes. Who else has a question? On the direct mail that I've gone out for on the first mailing, you were talking about $500 per thousand. But could that be in big quantities or considering it at small levels? Well, actually, I think it is a figure that I like to use that if your mailing, if your production expenses need to be less than that in big quantities for you to make, you need to work on improving the piece. Really, the cost of getting mailing out, it's like anything else. The more of it you do, the more letters you have printed, the less you're going to pay for any individual letter. And big companies like Phillips Publishing, they get out letters for $350 a thousand that it would cost me $502. So, but I think the 50 cents a letter is really a good rule of thumb, you know, because unless you're going over an ounce, you can get it down to 50 cents a letter most of the time, pretty close. Yeah, I'll tell you what I've done for that. I've done an eight-page letter with a penny attached, a business reply envelope, an order coupon and a lift letter, all for an ounce. This young lady, Terry Finder, stopped me on the break and told me that she had thought that she didn't know how this would apply, some of these things. But then last night, it kind of clicked, and I've asked her to read a little bit of a letter that she has written based on the work we did yesterday. Would you do that? I don't know how she should do that into a mic, Rocco, or she could just talk. Okay, they'll pick it up. Would you just read a little bit of your letter? What I said yesterday is I'm working on this project for CD rounds for colleges, meaning community or private or university. And so what my goal was to start the sales letter, what colleges want is more research and more enrollment. So I just patterned it exactly after the golf letter we did yesterday. Who else wants to double their lead almost immediately? Amazing new advertising idea created by 27-year-old Maureen can more than double your lead the first time you use it. Imagine one simple idea that effectively produces twice as many interests to prospect. Dear colleague, if the future of your job, and maybe even the college, depends on new sources for immediate enrollment, this will be the most important letter you will ever read. Here's what it's all about. My name is Jeff Hellerman, and until recently I was a full-time Maureen and a part-time artist. One of my hobbies was tinkering around with new graphics programs on my computer. I was always trying to figure out ways to make my designs more impactful. I tried all sorts of ideas until one day I hit jackpot. I swear you've never seen anything like this. It's so simple I can't believe somebody hasn't thought of this before. To tell the truth, I wouldn't have created it myself except that the company I now work for lost a college client quite unexpectedly. That left me with time on my hands and a lot of college advertising stuff in my computer's memory bay. That's when all of a sudden a question popped into my mind. How could you take advantage of all this material and create something that would outperform any individual piece by combining it all into one? So I did some research, and I studied the results of all the different materials. Here's what I found out. On the average, newspaper ads accounted for 15% of all leads. View votes accounted for 7%, course schedules 8%, free seminars 6%, direct mail 5%, the girls 32%, but guess what? The biggest source of media leads comes from TV. Why is this? Do you want to keep going? Yeah, I think it's great. Keep going. Well, television has characteristics that none of those other media can offer you. It has higher believability and it gets right in front of your prospect space that, listen to me, the television as it turns out has a fatal flaw. It produces tons of leads you convert probably at 25% or 30%. It produces tons of leads. You convert probably 25% or 30% of them, but only 2 thirds of those enroll in sticks. That means your default rates creep up to the danger zone and you start going ballistic. How does this happen? The truth is, it's inherent in the media. TV is believable and compelling, but it's not very selective. In other words, people who respond aren't necessarily qualified or interested enough in what you offer to hang in there. Once that made sense, I started to figure out how you take all the pulling power of television and the targeted selection characteristics of newspaper, direct mail, and combine them into a multimedia format. I wanted the user to be able to ask questions and have control over the direction of the information. But I also wanted all the answers to be provided through video interviews, not text. That's part of my gut. Let's give her a hand. I'm not going to laugh at this, but I think I learned the most yesterday. I revealed too much up front. I would reveal too much in headlines, but I've learned since I've been with this group that you taught us to hold back on telling us to keep the curiosity in there so that they want to ask. That's a very good piece of work. That's impressive. Now, I'm going to go through what I want to do is go through some of the things I promised to teach you and make some really brief comments on them. Some people here brought a really well put together book that has all of the bullets I put in the sales letter. I'm going to pick out the bullets that I think that you need to know right off the bat. I'm going to tell you the answers to that. Why the back end letters you write when you know my three main tricks may be 100 times more profitable than anything else you will ever write. Here's the first main trick. Your back end letter should go out instantly before your fulfillment package or before anything else. It should be a welcome letter that says thank you. You should give them post-purchase reassurance and you should ask them. You should give them an opportunity to buy more of what they've just bought, an improved version of it or something related to it. You should also, second trick is you should send that letter with a grabber attached to it and in most cases the best grabber you can use for that if it is a financially related product is a dollar bill. The third thing that you can do that will make that back end letter enormously profitable is send out that or follow up that memorable letter if it has a dollar attached or something to it a few days later with a telephone call. This secret weapon will make even the most skeptical of your prospects stand in line and beg you to take their money. There's a woman I mentioned in my newsletter and I mentioned at my seminars who is a complete cook but the best researcher I've ever met. Her name is Laura Charles. Her number is 216-229-1014 and she's used as a secret weapon by some of the biggest companies in America. Some of those companies are advertising agencies when they're going to pitch a client like Purdue, the guy who slaughters a third of all the chickens that are slaughtered in the United States. An advertising agency is going to go make a presentation to him. She does the research and teaches them how to talk chicken talk so that when they go talk to old man Purdue that they will be able to talk in a way that makes him think that they know more about chickens than they really do. It was a great documentary, 60 minutes or something on him, showed him walking amongst the mass of all these chickens. He told this real cute little yellow fuzzy chicken, said won't she just make a great chicken sandwich? I just thought he'd die. Okay. The Reporter of Direct Marketing. Yeah, as a matter of fact, Target would be good. Now, one of the things it says here is a truly no-brainer yet almost always overlooked way to bump up the size of your average order by 112% or more automatically. Actually, there's a couple of ways to do that. One of the simplest ways to do that is on your order coupon is to put in more than one order coupon. It's a very little known trick, but it almost always works. Instead of including one order coupon, include two. And then if that works, include three. And by the way, it's been tested and I don't know where it stops being effective, but it's not at seven. That's weird. I think if people really like your... I don't know for sure, but I think if people really like your letter, I think they give it to someone. Say, hey, you know, maybe you should read it. I think that it might. Don't send me cheats. Put in more than one order form in your letter. You might test that. Also, in the letter or in the order coupon, offer them the opportunity to buy more than one. Offer them the opportunity to buy a more expensive model. And if you're taking telephone orders, a no-brainer way to bump up your sales is to have the person say, by the way, Mr. Gillespie also has a tape on how to make money by following the Clydesdale horses around and picking up their cock-op. Would you like to have that? It's only an X. Whatever it is, it's one of the seven. And, you know, it's... It's nothing to say if it has no downside. The exact words you should use to write a money-back guarantee that increases sales and reduces refunds. What you should... One of the things that you should do if you're selling something that you really, really sure of is to tell them that you are... If they use this as instructed and they are not satisfied, they can get a double. They'll get twice their money-back. Now, the thing is, you have to be selling something of value. The other thing is they have to use it. I... Entrepreneur Magazine was selling these little how-to reports, they're actually kind of big reports, on how to start a balloon-vending business, how to open a floral shop. They were plagued by refunds. I got them to offer a double your money-back guarantee, but it was conditional. And it was the conditional that the person actually used the information. Now, the thing about that is that you can't put unfair burdens of proof on the person. And I did not. I just said, you know, send us a photocopy of your business license application. You know, one of your business cards and write us a letter requesting a refund on your letterhead. Now, that's not too much to ask for anybody to do. Here, you told me this years ago. It scared me to death because I write a newsletter and I offered double the money-back if they did not make at least $10,000 more next year after following my instructions. Now, after we've been doing this about almost three years now, the number of people that have asked for double their money-back is zero. The only people...and they have to wait a year. That was the contingent after one year. Some people ask for the money-back early on, but no one has double their money-back so far. And they'll call the department. And improve yourselves. Oh, absolutely. Gary, would you give a refund? And with that promotion, if someone refused to send that in but still asked for the refund, are you going to give them any way to... It depends. I have kind of taken a different tack on refunds than that I used to. When I'm selling to the general public and they don't know me or they don't know the client that I'm selling to, and, you know, if I'm selling like a $20 book or $30 bottle of perfume or something and they want a refund, I always advise the client to immediately get it, to give them the refund. But let me tell you something about refunds and consumerism. It used to be, and to some degree it still is true, that businesses exploit the consumer. More often nowadays, it's consumers that exploit the businessman. Do you know when people decide they want refunds? When they order. Most people know they are going to want a refund when they order. I...there's a gentleman here, bless God, that has found a book I wrote, and I didn't have any more copies of it called Thank God Not Everything Printed by the U.S. Government, News Is Its Value. It was a book on collecting and investing in C-tenant stamps. That book was written in the 1970s, and one of the people who ordered it was Alan Sean Feinstein, who publishes what he calls a newsletter, which is really a garbage-to-help sheet. He got my book, sent it back, and asked for a refund, because it wasn't what he expected. That was really kind of impossible, because that ad was not only blind. It told you what was in the book almost page for page. And then my book later appeared, the one he got the refund from in his newsletter. I mean, how cheeky can you be for Christ's sake, you know? So, what I think that you should honor all legitimate refunds, but I think you should stand firm about unfairness from the other side also. Okay? Tara? But you know, they, like some of the other teams, this is so expensive, they didn't give you the refund from the side of it. I don't mean to be unprojected about this thing. The FCC will not always be on their side. They will. They will not? No, they will not. Okay. Oh, by the way, and Pitt brought up something else. The longer the period, your refund period, the fewer refunds you will have. Nobody marks on their calendar 180 days forward last chance to get refunds from Gary Halberd Letter. You know, if the wait, the conventional thing to do, I'm only going to give them 10 days to apply for a refund. Well, you'll get more refunds because you've given them a deadline. And a general rule of human psychology is no one does anything until just before the deadline. That's right. This is it. Okay. How long do you wait to find out what your response rate is? You can send out the letters. And how long do you wait to count them? How long does that matter? As a general rule, and you can't rely on it completely, if you're mailing via first class and the response is coming by mail, the day that you deposit the mail at the post office should be counted as day one. And on day 12, you will have half of about, approximately half of what you're going to get, which is referred to in the industry as double day. The day you can double what you have so far to tell what you're going to get in the end. As a general rule, on the 21st day, you will have about 90% of all that you are going to get. And don't rely on that too closely because sometimes double day will occur faster than that. Sometimes double day will occur a little off that. But if you don't have good results in 21 days, you're not going to have good results. So it's pretty fast. No, those are days from the day you deposit the mail is the drop date. That's day one. And you count every day, including weekends. Let's see. Okay. Now here's something that's really important. Why you need to understand Guru Gary's porcupine theory that lets you find customers who are in heat for your product right now. A little known fact about porcupines is that the female only comes in heat about 15 minutes a year. If I were a porcupine, my significant other would have a headache during those 15 minutes. People go there buying desires like everything else about them goes in cycles. I believe that people who are interested in learning how to make more money have maybe, I'm not sure, an 18 month to a three month period of time when they avidly want information on how to make more money. And maybe at the end of that period of time or somewhere in there they will become discouraged and they are tougher to sell. Now, one of the things that you're getting that I didn't have to give the last attendees is a collection of advertising that was created by me and working with a guy named Sam Fishbine since the last seminar. Sam came here to the seminar. Sam Fishbine and his wife Leslie own five furniture stores, Casey Fine Furniture in Denver, Colorado. And he worked with me years ago and the way he worked with me is he paid me a lot of money to talk with him an hour every day on the phone and we just brainstorm. And then he would go type up the ads because he was running a newspaper ad a day. Now he still runs an enormous schedule like that. So he hired me again and he gets on the phone with me and we brainstorm and we produce ad after ad. This is one month's work. All these ads we did and they're just killers. I'm going to talk to you a little bit about some of them. But Sam is a guy who learned how to use a computer after he learned how to be a master copywriter. He sits there with a 20 inch screen and I swear to you at the end of some of our telephone sessions we have produced two ads that are typeset that are in my fax machine while I'm still on the phone and have been modem to the newspaper, ready to go. So one of the things that I want to say that Sam and I know is he's such a, he's so in tune that he's just a joy to work with is we're selling the same thing every ad that we write. You know he has quality furniture at good prices. But what we have to do is come up with a way to add drama and theater and showmanship to that and we have to find ways to reason why, reasons why that we are doing. Well the thing about all of the work that we do is that at any given period in time a certain segment of the population is in heat for furniture. And when they're not, they're not and it doesn't matter what you say. Doesn't matter. You know somebody's bought a house, they furnished it recently, it's full of furniture. It doesn't matter how clever or you know how fundamentally sound your advertising is. On the other hand, if they are in the market for furniture the person who has the most seductive bait of all those people in the ads are going to get this. So you need to know, and this is so simple, I'm almost embarrassed to explain it, how do you know when people are in heat for what you're selling? Well it comes back to what we talked about earlier this morning. If at all possible when you are renting a mailing list get the hotline names. And if you want to know a major secret of direct marketing success is to form a good relationship with your list broker and possibly the list owner and arrange to get him to rent you the names before he rents them to anything else. Remember that recency is a big factor. Someone who bought a diet book three years ago is not nearly as certain to be a hot prospect for a diet related product as someone who bought one last week. I'm sending it to everybody in the room but you. I'm sending you nothing. Okay. I'm going to talk to you about, there are differences that whisper and differences that scream. One of the problems we have in being successful in different areas of our life is that we major in minor things. An example of that would be trigonometry and Latin. Trigonometry and Latin have helped very few people in the room. Have they helped anybody? Nobody in the room. How many people studied either trigonometry or Latin? Yeah, look at all that. All that wasted time. And they made you major in a minor thing. How many of you have ever had any kind of problem in a relationship with the opposite sex? A girlfriend, the wife or anything? Have you ever had any kind of problem at all? What's the difference between doing that and doing that? Dip up. Squat, right? So one of the things I'm trying to do is to teach you to major in major things. But I will talk to you about some differences with whispers and I won't even slow down for you to take note. You can get this in a summary letter and you can get it in the tapes. Here's some differences that whisper. As a general rule, if you put the same amount of postage on an envelope with multiple stamps as you do with a single stamp, your response will increase. As a general rule, if you use a commemorative stamp, a large commemorative stamp instead of a regular first class stamp, you will improve your response. As a general rule, if you print your letters one side only instead of front back, front back. In other words, if you have a four page letter, instead of using two sheets of paper, you use four pages, it will increase response. As a general rule, adding as though it were a mistake, one or more extra order coupons in the letter will increase response. As a general rule, and this is one that's important, the sturdier your envelope, you should not economize on envelopes, the better quality of envelopes that you have, the better response that you have. As a general rule, if you will go through your letters and highlight significant things with a yellow highlighter pen and give them to a printer who can reproduce that, then you will increase your response. As a general rule, if you sign your letters with a blue signature, it will increase response. Let's see, what else whispers? It's not so much it whispers. What temperature should the room be when you're writing? It should be 67 degrees. The reason for that is that if it's too warm, you'll be lethargic, if it's too cold, your body will be struggling to overcome that. What you want is to be invigorating. The kind of music that you want to listen to when you are writing, this is a little more important than you might guess it is, is you want to listen to Baroque music. That's classical music like Mozart. You don't want any power classics. There's an album called Power Classics. You don't want to be trying to write, you don't want that. I'm doing it in the background. And by the way, there's a, don't know what to buy, what's that one I use? Classical music for idiots. It's classical music for idiots. And they give you enough of the classics of each classic so that you know whether you like this or not. But get some nice Baroque gifts. It was about 60 minutes where I saw where, just in Mozart's action-stimulating freeway activities, a jumble-going test with the college students that was in the 10 minutes, and had worked for the 10 minutes of Mozart's writing and taking the past work, which I hire on the album, and I had to listen to it. It's very mathematical, it's all very orderly, it's great stuff. Now, we're going to answer as the wrap-up of today's session. Now, we've got a little while. I don't have to answer the most important question yet. Yes. You're going to talk about differences in screen? Yes. That's what I just decided to do. Here are differences that screen. The number one most screaming difference of all is the mailing list that you use. The wrong mailing list doesn't matter. Nothing else counts. If you are selling something to nurses and somehow you mail that promotion to secretaries, it won't work. If you sell a book on how to lose body fat to a list of anorexics, it doesn't matter how clever your copy is. I actually was bitch-dead by the anorexics of America, believe it or not. I never even knew there was such a group. I wonder if there's any federal, state, local agency I have not offended in some way. I'd like to find out if there is. Well, you probably know my feeling about midgets because they're my only prejudice. I do not like midgets and dwarfs. They're the only group of people I'm genuinely prejudiced against. The thing is, if you get pissed off at a midget, the only thing you can do is stab him in the top of the head. It's your only option. Other people you have... Shut up. You're in the room with a sick man. I think so, right? Okay. The mailing list is the first difference in the section. Okay. The next difference that screams will either be the headline or if you're not using the headline, it's the same principle. It's the first sentence of your letter. The headline of your letter, the headline of your ad is absolutely crucial. If you miss there, you know, you're going to mess up all the rest of it. Another category of differences that screams is showmanship and grabbers. I cannot overemphasize the importance of grabbers. Grabbers are something that grabs the attention that you attach to the first page of a letter that grabs your recipient's attention and causes him to grab for his wallet or the phone. Here are some really good grabbers. Money. I have been responsible for so many letters when going out with a penny attached. They have had to mint more because of things. Literally, mint more and ship them in box copies. I often send dollar bill letters for a very famous financial newsletter writer Dick Young. I went to Tijuana and gathered up pesos and came back and wrote my famous peso letter that mailed for years. The thrust of that was that the Mexican peso was once a rock-hard currency that traded, I think, an eight or sixteen to the dollar, and now it takes a wheelbarrow full of them to buy a loaf of bread. It was a great grab. For Joe Carbo, who made most of his money selling how to beat the horses, and actually as opposed to Lazy Man's Way to Rich's, I went to a racetrack and picked up losing tickets and attached the ticket to the first page of the letter. For another thing for Carbo, on one of his racing systems, I attached a two dollar bill. The premises, we're so sure this system is going to help you beat the horses. Here's two bucks to make your first bet with. When I went to Cuba last year, what I tried to do and I just didn't have enough time to pull it out, I got to be the only person in the world that wants worthless Cuban pesos. There's three kinds of money in Cuba. Worthless Cuban pesos, American dollars, and certificates that are exchangeable one-to-one for a dollar. And I want, everybody's trying to dump all these pesos and I want it because I want to attach a Cuban peso to a letter and tell a story about them. I don't know what the story will be, but I think that will really be a grabber. Foreign money is a good grabber. Another good grabber is photographs. Do you know, just think about this. Suppose you get a letter from someone asking you to do business with them and nothing whatsoever is changed about the letter, except that there is what appears to be a real photograph of the letter writer and it says, Dear Mr. Everett, I'm not sure that we have ever met, but I know that we kind of have a common interest. Just in case I'm wrong, I've sent a photograph of myself which was taken last month at the XYZ convention where I understand you attended also. I'm doing this just in case you may have seen me and besides that, I think it's always sort of nice that people have an idea of what the person looks like when they do business with them. Anyhow, here's why I'm writing. I would add something to it, wouldn't you? Put a photograph of a beautiful woman on the letter and find some reason to do that. There was this guy who was an ego maniac who ran ads in the Wall Street Journal, how to pay zero taxes, showed him, the ad showed him, and all these law books behind him sitting at the desk very profit. Photographer friend of his convinced him to put a very voluptuous blonde model in the ad with him. She had no reason to be in the ad. She didn't even look like she was his secretary or anything. It increased the response phenomenally and he never used it again because he thought that it didn't fit with his image. Another guy wrote a, now this was a more direct relationship, was written a report on massage. I suggested that we make it the art of erotic massage. Then I suggested that he put a picture of a woman in it. At that time we used a picture of a woman who was my girlfriend. The copy was, can you imagine spending a night with this woman? That could be a woman like this. It could be the most exciting night of your life or it could be the most embarrassing. So now let me tell you something about photographs of women. In advertising, men like to look at photographs of women and women like to look at photographs of women. Seldom, does anyone like to look at photographs of men? There's almost no market of all for male models. I would caution you to be very, very careful when you use a photograph of a woman in your promotions and not make it extraordinarily sexy. What you should do is make, you should use photographs of women who are visually exciting. And the most attractive thing about a woman that a man is really interested in is her face. Believe it or not, in spite of all the pigs in the room, that's really, really true. I got a great picture of my cat. He made us a lot of money trying to help us. Is she blonde? She got blue eyes? Yeah, but she just has a great barrel in that room. She looks like she was from Wisconsin's corn fed, doesn't she? So that you will know, the most popular model type in the world is blonde blue-eyed corn fed girls from the Midwest. My photographer friend Don Camp blew into town two weeks ago with a chic and his entourage. The chic was worth $3 billion or $6 billion. And he said, Gary, I just want to add a little excitement to his life. We're having lunch at the China Grill in 90 minutes. Can you show up with some models? I said, sure, so I did. And then he wanted to do something that evening. So I rented a limousine and, you know, Anna, the girl that was here yesterday and took her and her roommate up there. And Anna, these girls are really very nice girls. They're not what chic's wish they would be. But this guy has done everything he can. He calls her from Abu Dhabi or wherever he's at. You know, I mean, she's big, she's tall, she's blonde, she's beautiful, she's got blue eyes and she's got this guy with $3 billion that would do anything. And we just laugh about it, you know. But I did introduce him to another blue-eyed blonde and he did send her a $6,000 playing ticket and she flew over there a couple of days ago. So what's that got to do with that? She's six feet tall, her roommate is six feet long. It was kind of neat I had my stepdaughter Natalie come down from five feet tall. And I took her to dinner and I had her girlfriend from Carthage, New York come to. Jessica, Jessica's five foot seven. It was just wonderful having her here, you know. For one thing, Jessica being from Carthage, New York had never had a real haircut before, only had given to her mother. So I took her one of the top salons in South Beach and had the little gay guys all make sure they all fluttered around her and cut her hair. And it was delightful. I just bought her a little dress and I took them to the strand for dinner. And Natalie was saying, I just feel so small, you know, walking beside Jessica. I said, oh honey, don't worry, Jessica will feel so too tall after this. First of all, you know, we're living in South Beach. Everybody's 30 years older or 30 years younger than me. They're all a foot and a half taller, foot and a half shorter. But anyway, so there's all these, but anyhow, we're having this and then Anna joined us for dinner. And then she comes walking with her six inch heels. There's Natalie and over there. But they got. Different things that screen photographs, you can use as a graphic. Here is a very curious one. You all know about tear sheet men's where people mail a what appears to be a newspaper ad or was a newspaper ad. They have a yellow tear sheet attached. They have a yellow posted notes attached to it and says, Roy, really good information, Jay, or something like that. I'm trying to find one of these with the tear sheet. Well, Kyle. And he has had a client. And this guy asked him. They got, they just, they took a regular printed letter. And they attached a post it note to the front sheet of the letter and wrote on it, you know, Roy, great info, Jay or whatever it was. Oh, here's one. Okay. We did this thing. We did so. And then I arranged this one says Richard great info are now I did a five. thousand piece test. But just attaching the posted note to it. So and that makes an exponential increase in response as opposed to a printed letter. Who knows why. But but in fact, it does. They're giving somebody a hard time for mailing tear sheets and some big company Xerox or somebody. But a post, by the way, when you use a post it note, make the initial, the same initial of a person who's writing the letter. So if they say who's are, says Richard Stanton, Joe. A difference that absolutely bellows is your guarantee. As a general rule, he who has the strongest guarantee wins. Gene, come on up and talk. What he doesn't have to come up. Gene has a new grabber. And I think it's a killer. And I'm going to let him try and sell some of them to you. I said nothing to be sold at this seminar, but this is a hot one. I'm going to buy it myself.
